data.title: SL Green exceeds initial $1.0B fundraising goal for Opportunistic Debt Fund
data.description:
SL Green Realty (NYSE:SLG), Mahattan's largest office landlord, has topped its preliminary $1.0B fundraising goal for a discretionary fund launched last year, it said on Thursday.

The SLG Opportunistic Debt Fund has received more than $500.0M in new capital commitments this week alone, with several additional closings expected to occur soon.

The discretionary fund focuses on capitalizing on the dislocation between rapidly improving leasing fundamentals and the early stages of improving debt capital markets, targeting high-quality assets in New York City,

The fund is being actively deployed into structured debt investments sourced through long-standing sponsor and lender relationships, and proprietary networks. It will originate new loans and/or buy existing loans, loan portfolios and controlling CMBS securities.

Public pensions, insurance companies, and select high-net-worth platforms were among the global institutional investors backing the fund.

SLG shares dipped 3.9% in midafternoon trading, a day after the REIT posted stronger guidance and better-than-expected Q2 earnings and revenue.

data.title: S&P 500 and Nasdaq top record highs after retail sales and further earnings data
data.description:
Wall Street trades higher on Thursday, with both the Nasdaq Composite and S&P 500 surging to fresh record highs as investors weighed new data on retail sales and jobless claims.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has pushed up by 0.6% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) has climbed by 0.6%. At the same time, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has advanced by 0.8%. 

Among the 11 S&P 500 sectors, nine are up in the green, with Info Tech and Financials leading the way. At the other end of the spectrum, Health Care has been the area that has suffered the most so far. 

U.S. Treasury yields observe limited moves with the shorter-end U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) up 2 basis points at 3.92% and the longer-end U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) has risen 1 basis point to 4.46%. 

Looking out at the economic calendar and June retail sales came in at +0.6% M/M to $720.1B vs. +0.1% consensus and -0.9% prior (unrevised), while core retail sales, excluding motor vehicles and parts, came in at +0.5% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and -0.6% prior (revised from -0.3%). 

“Strong June retail sales show consumers may have been spooked by tariffs, but they haven't fully gone into hiding. The fact that June is a slower month and that goods outlays comprise a fraction of overall outlays means that we are not completely rethinking our forecast for a soft second half for consumer spending,” Wells Fargo’s Tim Quinlan said. 

Furthermore, initial jobless claims unexpectedly fell in the past week. Business inventories also held steady again in May at 0.0% M/M to $2,656.7B. Additionally, homebuilder sentiment ticked up one point in July, as was expected. 

“The VIX eased slightly overnight. Despite the back-and-forth headlines concerning the possible sacking of Fed Chair Powell and President Trump’s trade wars, market participants ultimately didn’t panic, leaving implied volatility apparently well anchored. The muted reaction to yesterday’s events suggests that traders remain focused on earnings and macro data rather than political theatre, at least for now,” David Morrison of Trade Nation said. 

Earnings remain in focus, with Travelers Corp, Citizens Financial and US Bancorp reporting. Netflix will headline after hours, offering a tech-sector litmus test amid rising subscriber growth expectations, Morrison added. 

Streaming giant Netflix (NFLX) will release its quarterly results after the closing bell.

data.title: Steve Madden gains a bull at Citigroup on shifting trends, 'dupes' popularity
data.description:
The trend back to dress shoes will benefit Steve Madden (NASDAQ:SHOO), says Citigroup, with revenue expected to increase 12% next year thanks to the company’s shoe selection and contributions from its Kurt Geiger acquisition in February.

Steve Madden (NASDAQ:SHOO) shares have capitulated to increased costs tied to tariffs and reallocated discretionary spending, shedding 38% so far this year. But the worst may be behind the brand, thanks to shifting fashion trends and as social media attention drives demand for its designer-inspired “dupes.”

As tariff pressures ease thanks to a pivot away from China-based manufacturing, and fashion trends favoring Steve Madden’s (NASDAQ:SHOO) offerings, Citigroup moved into the bullish camp, raising its rating on the stock to Buy from Neutral and hiking its price target by 23% to $32, a 30% premium to Wednesday’s closing price.

The firm now expects FY26 earnings of $2.15 per share versus the consensus estimate of $1.94 per share, and gross margins exceeding 40%.  

The upgrade gave a boost to Steve Madden (SHOO) stock, trading nearly 7% higher into Thursday’s close.

data.title: Travelers signals $700M share buybacks in 2026 as capital management and segment growth drive strong Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Travelers Companies, Inc. (TRV) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Alan David Schnitzer, Chairman and CEO, opened the call stating, "We are pleased to report exceptional second quarter results, driven by excellent underwriting and investment performance. We earned core income of $1.5 billion or $6.51 per diluted share." Schnitzer highlighted a core return on equity of 18.8% for the quarter and 17.1% for the trailing 12 months. He emphasized a nearly 10-point improvement in the reported combined ratio to 90.3%, with all segments contributing to strong net earned premiums and profitability. Schnitzer announced, "In May, we announced an agreement to sell most of our Canadian business to Definity for $2.4 billion or 1.8x book value, excluding excess local capital." He stated that about $700 million of the net cash proceeds is expected to be allocated for additional share repurchases in 2026, and the transaction is expected to be slightly accretive to earnings per share in the next several years.

  • Daniel Stephen Frey, CFO, reported, "We generated higher earned premiums and meaningfully improved both the reported and underlying combined ratios compared to the prior year quarter." Frey noted after-tax net investment income of $774 million for the quarter and operating cash flows of $2.3 billion. He detailed a year-to-date expense ratio of 28.4% and reaffirmed the full-year expectation of 28% to 28.5%. "We returned $809 million of capital to our shareholders this quarter, comprising share repurchases of $557 million and dividends of $252 million."

  • Gregory Cheshire Toczydlowski, President of Business Insurance, shared, "Business Insurance had a very strong second quarter, delivering segment income of $813 million, up nearly 25% from the prior year quarter, driven by higher net earned premiums and a combined ratio that improved 2.5 points from the prior year quarter to a terrific 93.6%."

  • Jeffrey Peter Klenk, President of Bond & Specialty Insurance, reported, "We generated segment income of $244 million and an outstanding combined ratio of 80.3% in the quarter."

  • Michael Frederick Klein, President of Personal Insurance, stated, "Personal Insurance delivered segment income of $534 million for the second quarter of 2025. The combined ratio of 88.4% improved 20 points relative to the prior year quarter, driven by a decrease in catastrophe losses of nearly 14 points and a 7-point improvement in the underlying combined ratio."

Outlook

  • Frey stated, "Our outlook for fixed income NII, including earnings from short-term securities, has increased from the outlook we provided a quarter ago. We now expect approximately $770 million after tax in the third quarter and $805 million after tax in the fourth quarter." Schnitzer expressed, "With our diversified business operating from a position of strength, our outlook for continued premium growth at attractive underwriting margins, orderly conditions generally in our target markets and a positive trajectory for investment income, we remain highly confident in the outlook for our business."

Financial Results

  • The company reported net earned premiums of $10.9 billion, net written premiums of $11.5 billion, and after-tax net investment income of $774 million. Catastrophe losses totaled $927 million pretax. The reported combined ratio improved to 90.3%, and the underlying combined ratio was 84.7%. Core income for the quarter was $1.5 billion or $6.51 per diluted share, with a core return on equity of 18.8%. Operating cash flows reached $2.3 billion, and adjusted book value per share ended the quarter at $144.57.

Q&A

  • Charles Gregory Peters, Raymond James: Asked about pricing pressure and market competition in Business Insurance and the impact on Select and Middle Market. Toczydlowski clarified that pricing pressure was most relevant to large National Property and had minimal impact on Select. Schnitzer added, "Price in National Property was lower. Workers' comp pricing was about the same, but price change in every other line... was actually quite strong."

  • Peters also inquired about the relaxation of personal lines restrictions. Klein responded that most actions to relax property restrictions should be completed by the end of 2025, which could drive both property and auto growth.

  • David Kenneth Motemaden, Evercore: Asked about durability of property pricing outside National Property. Schnitzer replied, "The overall landscape is very positive and really consistent with what we're seeing in terms of returns... property outside of National Property has just performed differently, as you'd expect."

  • Motemaden asked if favorable weather aided the Business Insurance loss ratio. Frey responded, "We saw a little bit of favorability again in this year's quarter... plus or minus 1 point, the combined ratio is still outstanding in Business Insurance."

  • Brian Robert Meredith, UBS: Asked about tort inflation impact. Schnitzer replied, "The tort inflation is alive and well, hasn't gone anywhere and continues to show up in the numbers. I would say we've got an expectation for it, we're pricing for it."

  • Meyer Shields, KBW: Asked about adverse selection in personal auto new business. Klein said, "We don't see any signs of adverse selection in our profile metrics for either the business we're retaining or the new business we're writing."

  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the impact of tariffs. Schnitzer said, "We really haven't seen really any impact of tariffs across any of our businesses, not -- certainly not in any meaningful way."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally neutral to slightly positive, focusing on pricing, profitability, and the sustainability of favorable trends, but probing for risks related to inflation, tariffs, and competition.

  • Management maintained a confident and occasionally defensive tone, especially when addressing market cycles and inflation, repeatedly emphasizing strong execution, rational market conditions, and robust capital management. Schnitzer stated, "We are relentless in our commitment to disciplined capital allocation and value creation."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained focused on pricing, inflation, and catastrophe trends, but management displayed increased confidence due to improved combined ratios and investment income.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The sale of the Canadian business and the forthcoming $700 million share repurchase allocation for 2026 are major new developments.

  • Net earned and written premiums grew across all segments, with segment income and combined ratios showing notable improvement. The previous quarter was heavily impacted by California wildfire catastrophe losses, while Q2 benefited from lower catastrophe impact and improved profitability.

  • Management's tone this quarter is more optimistic, emphasizing momentum and capital management, while analysts continue to revisit inflation, pricing, and risk questions.

  • No major shifts in strategic focus, but the Canadian sale illustrates a renewed emphasis on optimizing capital allocation and long-term value creation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing tort and social inflation risks, but stated these are reflected in pricing and reserving.

  • Competitive pressures were noted in property pricing for large National Accounts, but overall market conditions are described as rational.

  • Tariffs and legislative impacts have not had a material effect but remain on the radar.

  • Catastrophe losses, while lower this quarter, are still a factor and are closely managed through reinsurance.

Final Takeaway

Travelers delivered strong second-quarter results, underpinned by improved underwriting margins, robust investment income, and disciplined capital management. The announced sale of the Canadian business and the plan to allocate $700 million of proceeds for share repurchases in 2026 highlight the company's ongoing commitment to shareholder value. Management remains confident in the company’s outlook, citing continued premium growth, favorable market conditions, and further investment income gains, while maintaining a vigilant stance toward inflation and competitive pressures.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Peacock's latest price hike makes its ad-supported plan the costliest vs. rival streamers
data.description:
Peacock, the NBCU-owned (NASDAQ:CMCSA) streaming platform, will hike its prices for new subscribers by $3 per month, starting July 23, according to a report from Vulture on Thursday.

The report said the cost of the ad-supported plan would go up 40% to $10.99, and the price for the ad-free plan would go up 20% to $16.99. Existing subscribers will be billed as per the new rates on or after August 22.

The monthly cost for the Peacock subscription with ads is currently the highest when compared to rival streaming platforms.

Max (WBD), Hulu, and Disney+ (DIS) with ads are priced at $9.99; Prime Video (AMZN) standalone plan costs $8.99; and the ad-supported tiers of Netflix (NFLX) and Paramount+ (PARA) (PARAA) are $7.99 each.

The price increase by NBCU comes after its $2 hike last year and is also due to the hefty investment in sports content—particularly the NBA, coming on the platform in October, for which it is shelling out nearly $27B over 11 years.

data.title: ADM, Ingredion slip after Trump says Coke will stop using corn syrup in U.S.
data.description:
Shares of corn syrup suppliers Archer Daniels Midland (NYSE:ADM) and Ingredion (NYSE:INGR) -1% and -1.1% in Thursday's trading, respectively, paring earlier declines of more than 6%, after President Trump said Coca-Cola (KO) would return to using cane sugar in its namesake soda.

Coca-Cola (KO) did not confirm the decision but said in a statement that "more details on new innovative offerings within our Coca-Cola product range will be shared soon."

Health and Human Services secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who has vowed to "Make America Healthy Again," has called high fructose corn syrup bad for Americans' health and "a formula for making you obese and diabetic."

The Corn Refiners Association trade group warned about potential job losses that could follow a Coca-Cola (KO) switch, arguing that "replacing high fructose corn syrup with cane sugar would cost thousands of American food manufacturing jobs, depress farm income, and boost imports of foreign sugar, all with no nutritional benefit."

U.S. sugar futures (SB1:COM) rose on the news, with the most-active October contract +1.1% in choppy trading.

data.title: British American Tobacco on track to break six-session rally
data.description:
British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) looked set to end a six-day winning streak as they moved into the red territory on Thursday. BTI lost 1.91% to trade at $ $51.27 during afternoon trading.

The stock has been rallying since July 9, when it ended 2.42% higher at $49.09 after Jefferies initiated coverage on the company with a Buy rating and named the stock its top pick in the tobacco sector.

Since then, BTI has gained 6.48% compared to a 0.01% rise in the broader markets. On a year-to-date basis, BTI has grown by over 12%, while in the last year it has advanced by more than 30%.

As per Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings, it is recommended to Hold the stock. It has a score of 3.31 out of 5, with an A+ grade for profitability and momentum but a D for growth. Wall Street analysts echoed the same sentiments and issued a Hold rating for BTI. However, Seeking Alpha analysts have a Buy call for the stock.

SA analyst The Asian Investor has assigned a Strong Buy rating to the stock, describing it as ‘a compelling value investment’, citing strong capital returns fueled by robust free cash flow and a low earnings-based valuation.

The analyst also argued that BTI’s growing momentum in alternative products like Velo is helping offset declines in traditional tobacco, thereby supporting future growth in a challenging market.

“Aggressive marketing and ambitious medium-term goals in smoke-free products position BTI for sustained top line and earnings momentum,” they added.

Sensor Unlimited also reiterated its Buy rating for the stock following the company’s trade update released in early June.

“After examining the updates, I keep seeing a biased reward/risk curve despite the large price rally since my last writing. The leading catalysts in my view are the progress its New Category products have reported and its capital allocation choices,” the analyst noted.

data.title: Cypto thefts hit $2.17B so far in 2025, exceeding all 2024 losses: Chainalysis
data.description:
More than $2.17B has been stolen from crypto services and individual wallets so far this year, already surpassing 2024's total losses, according to a report published Thursday.

Stolen crypto funds are projected to potentially hit $4B by the end of 2025, underscoring how "the stakes for cybersecurity have never been higher." blockchain intelligence company Chainalysis said.

Notably, crypto exchange Bybit's $1.5B hack made up most (69%) of the total service losses YTD as of June.

In Q1, hackers stole $1.67B, a 303% surge from the previous quarter, blockchain security firm CeriK had noted. The Bybit attack occurred in Q1.

"At $1.5 billion, this single incident not only represents the largest crypto theft in history, but also accounts for approximately 69% of all funds stolen from services this year," Chainalysis said.

More recently, it added, theft from personal wallets is becoming a larger slice of total crypto losses, with attacks on individual users accounting for about 23% of all stolen funds this year.
